Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd spend maybe 30 hours a week on coursework, but it was scattered and unfocused. I'd often sit down intending to study but end up just kind of skimming through things without really diving deep. Some weeks I'd cram a lot in, other weeks barely anything. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't get myself to stick to a schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd study when I felt like it or when something was due soon, not on any set schedule. Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving consistently. If nothing was pressing, I'd drift off and do other things instead. I'm more of a go-with-the-flow type, which doesn't always work well for academics.
Q3: Usually it was because something was due or I'd gotten a notification about a deadline. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new, but I wouldn't stay long. If I heard from a classmate that something important was posted, that would get me to log in too. It wasn't really a habit, more like a reaction to external pressure.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. It never became automatic for me. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I logged in more regularly because we had a lot of assignments, but once that passed, I'd go back to sporadic use. I just couldn't make it stick as part of my daily routine. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do.
Q5: I'm an auditory learner, so the video lectures were actually pretty helpful when I watched them. I racked up a decent amount of watch time because I could listen while doing other things. The discussion boards were okay too because I could hear different perspectives from classmates. Anything I could listen to rather than read worked better for me.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and a lot of the optional stuff. If it wasn't going to be graded, I probably didn't touch it. Some resources just seemed redundant or too dense for what I needed. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came. The textbook readings especially just sat there untouched.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to passing the course, it felt worth my time. Like, if I knew something would be on the exam or counted toward my grade, I'd engage with it. Anything that felt like busywork or extra credit I just couldn't motivate myself to care about. I needed to see the point immediately or I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely different. Required activities had this weight to them, like I had no choice. Optional ones? I'd look at them and think 'maybe later' and then never do them. There was no consequence for skipping optional stuff, so my brain just filed it away as unimportant. I know that's not the best approach, but that's how it felt.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. Sometimes I'd study with friends in person and we'd talk through concepts together. I also used some YouTube videos to explain things the course materials didn't make clear. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: When I was listening to downloaded lectures while walking around or when I studied with friends over coffee, none of that showed up anywhere. We'd quiz each other verbally, talk through problems, and that was genuinely helpful. But the LMS would just show I hadn't logged in for days. The system couldn't see any of that real engagement.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download a video or PDF once, then never open it on the platform again. I'd have it saved on my laptop or phone and refer back to it multiple times. The LMS would only register that one initial access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times offline. It's just more convenient that way.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to care. Mid-semester was rough when everything piled up. I had extracurriculars demanding my time, and the course material started feeling more abstract and disconnected. My attendance dropped during those weeks. I wasn't anxious about it exactly, I just felt kind of numb toward the whole thing.
Q13: Yeah, for sure. When I had a lot going on with my other activities, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. Fatigue was a big one too. After a long day, the last thing I wanted was to log in and watch another lecture. Sometimes I was just confused about what I was supposed to be doing, and that made me avoid the platform entirely.
Q14: Plenty of times. I'd open a video and let it play while I scrolled through my phone or zoned out. Technically I was 'watching' but nothing was sinking in. Same with discussion posts. I'd skim through them without really absorbing what people were saying. My body was there but my mind was somewhere else entirely.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but I'd keep pushing it back. Sometimes the task just felt overwhelming and I didn't know where to start. Other times I'd convince myself I work better under pressure, so I'd wait until the last minute. It's a bad habit but I kept falling into it throughout the semester.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd cram everything in right before due dates. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the days before assignments were due and flatlined the rest of the time. Without deadlines, I'd just drift. They were stressful but also the only thing keeping me somewhat on track.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got it, I'd already mentally moved on to the next thing. If I got good feedback, I'd feel briefly okay about it. Bad feedback just made me want to avoid thinking about the course. I didn't really use it to improve my approach going forward, which I know isn't great.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty loose to me, which wasn't ideal for someone like me who needs external pressure. With 11 online courses to juggle, this one often fell to the bottom of my priorities. If there had been more frequent check-ins or required participation, I probably would have engaged more consistently. The flexibility worked against me.
Q19: The video content was accessible, which helped since I learn by listening. But the sheer amount of material across all my courses made it hard to keep up. Having so many resources available sounds good but it was actually overwhelming. I never knew what was truly essential versus just supplementary, so I'd often just not engage at all.
Q20: I can totally see how that happens. You might be logging in, clicking around, watching videos, but never actually completing the assignments. Maybe you're trying to understand the material but struggling to produce anything. Or you're procrastinating by feeling busy without actually being productive. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That's kind of me, honestly. My exam score wasn't great but I passed. A lot of my learning happened offline through downloaded materials and talking with friends. The LMS doesn't capture conversations or the times I listened to lectures while walking. You can learn without constantly being logged into a platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss offline studying completely. They also can't capture the quality of engagement, just the quantity. Someone could spend hours on the platform and learn nothing, or barely log in and still get the concepts.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. For me, engagement happened in bursts when something genuinely interested me or when pressure forced me to focus. It's about your mind being present, not just your browser being open.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually do care about learning on some level, even if my behavior doesn't always reflect it. It wouldn't capture my conversations with classmates or the times I genuinely wrestled with concepts in my head. It also wouldn't show that I went through CBT sessions to try to manage my stress and approach things differently. There's a whole internal experience that data just can't capture.
